speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 6. Today's date is June 26, 1996. My name is Joe Bobrow. The survivor is Marion Gottesman, and we're in Regal Park, New York. But we were talking about Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where I'd like to start again.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there's not so much to talk about it anymore, except that waiting around, which I told you already, and story about my mother, I told you, too. And the only other thing was, like, one day was appell, and they took 200 people. And we were-- they counted out five by five. We were 95-- we were-- no, we were 100 people, not 200 people. We were 100 people. And we were in a row of 90, 95, then came 100, and the rest of them stayed, and they took us and walked us through another sauna again. And on that walk, walking from that one camp into the other one, all I could think of is, now we're going to go into the gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back. How long were you in Auschwitz, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately between two and four weeks. I don't know exact time, because there was no time for us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask, how did you keep track of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. This is why I never know how long I was in Auschwitz, because there-- you lived from one appell to the other, from one-- whatever you could eat when they gave us something to eat, and that was it. And you don't have-- you lose complete track of, of day, night, and, and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you thinking about your father-- what happened to him? Is this something that was on your minds during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't want to acknowledge it. We did not want to acknowledge that the people-- like my father, I mean, to me, was still a young man. He was 50 years old, right? That they would kill people like that on the spur of the moment, taking them off the train, right into the gas. But that's what happened. I mean, all that acceptance, I believe, came later, of that. But knowing that there were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the ovens burning, and you did see from a distance the smokes and et cetera, and had an idea what was going on, that's what I've come to now, because when they took us from-- the 100 of us and took us to another sauna, which we cleaned in place-- that walk, I will never forget, because I thought, this is it. We're going to be gassed. And I remember exactly what-- and I still talk about it with my friends-- what I felt. I felt, all right, when it happen, it happen. It's not me alone. I'm with my friends and with my mother. That was my, my own, you know, feeling. It wouldn't matter, because I was not alone. I didn't think of how it was done, or-- there was no after anymore. It was just a fact. Now we're going to die. And then it was a surprise. They were-- took us through the sauna again, gave us some different clothes. I don't remember exactly what it was-- a little bit-- maybe a little bit warmer, I don't know, but still with the, the wooden shoes. And they brought us to a train station. And over there, I had another experience. I run into two girls. They were twins who were, what they called in Auschwitz, [GERMAN], meaning they were sent from one barrack to the other to take care of the kapos, doing things for the kapos. And I don't know-- one was called [GERMAN], and the other one was [GERMAN], whatever that meant over there. And those two girls were with us in Theresienstadt together and had come to Auschwitz a few weeks before us. But there also was-- which we didn't know at the time-- under control from Mengele visits, experiments, et cetera. And when they saw me, and when they saw the other girls which they know-- we were, like, quite a few girls there together, which, you know, we were all together in Theresienstadt-- one of them came and brought me a scarf to put around my neck or head or whatever. I met them again here in New York, and one of them committed suicide right here in New York. She was married and had children, and she committed suicide. I think it must be about 15, 20 years ago. And the other one lives in Boston. So I guess it had the aftereffect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me again, in Auschwitz, how did you know that the chimney and the smoke going up--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were walking into the camp, we had guards next to us. And first of all, what I remember-- I didn't mention it before-- there was, like, behind the wires, there was one place, all you saw were children carriages, baby carriages. And I asked the guard next to me, I said, how come there are so many carriages? [INAUDIBLE] very easy, because all the children went into the gas, and this is most likely where you'll end up, too. That's how I know that they were gassed. And then somebody told us, the chimneys, when you see the chimneys burning, that's where the people are. That's how we knew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I saw the same thing, like with the, the carriages, the baby carriages. We saw one place, all walking into the barracks, one big place, only with shoes. That was in the open, you know? They had them all collected, and most likely sent away, or did things with them. I don't know. How many shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thousands. Thousands. Like, if you would have a, a baseball field, right? Full of shoes-- that's how it looked to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: All gathered together?

speaker: interviewee
text: All gathered together, mountains of shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Piled up high?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mountains of shoes. Mountains of baby carriages. This is how I know. That's what I remember walking in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where the gas chambers were? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw-- we didn't really know. But they told us, when you see the smoke from the chimneys, this is where they burn the people. That, that's how we knew. When we saw the, the chimneys smoking, that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any names of the commanders of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at the time, at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all-- no, none, none, till we found out that Mengele was the one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out it was Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was in Merzdorf. [BACKROUND NOISE] I didn't think we-- I don't think we knew before, because when he came that time, and he recognized my mother and me, and we were working in-- you know, this was a factory, where we were. And we even slept in the factory. They had two floors. One floor was for the SS, and one floor was for us, right? And then we were behind doors, and, and, and gates and things like that at night. They locked us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of the guards there, right? I don't know. My mother worked in a different place than I did, so she was working more outside than I did. I worked right in the factory. So what happened is, she must have, not knowing the guard, but talked to the guard. And she asked who that SS man was, and it was Mengele. So-- Was his name well-known already? I mean, once--

speaker: interviewee
text: It must-- I guess so. I don't know. I mean, this is the first time that I had any knowledge of Mengele--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --except that I saw him in Auschwitz, had the experience with him there, and later on in, in Merzdorf. But I didn't know it was the Dr. Mengele who became that famous afterwards, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were mentioning, you walked-- you remember walking from Auschwitz to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just to the-- where the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the cattle trains, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a far walk? Was it difficult? [SIGHS] Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't that far. It was not really far.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 100--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --100 women?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 100 women, and then there were-- we were 100 women, and we were called to Czech transport, because we came from Czechoslovakia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the same trains, there were about three other-- what do you call it-- wagons there. There were 200-- that's where I got messed up. There were 200 Hungarian girls. They were in, in another cattle car. And then there were some men, two, two cars full of men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what they did, they brought us from Auschwitz to Gross-Rosen, and in Gross-Rosen, they let the men out. They stayed in-- they went to Gross-Rosen. I don't know what happened to them afterwards. And the 200 Hungarian girls and us, the Czechs, we went into Merzdorf, not knowing where we would end up, but that's where we-- What-- and you were in cattle cars again? Was it crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean take, 100 people in one car, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it is a bad as the ride to Auschwitz? How would you compare?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the, the ride to Auschwitz was worse. To me, it was worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was similar? It was no bathrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No bathrooms, no nothing, except they had a, a little room in the cattle car, where they had, like, a guard. And the guards were not the real SS. At that time, they had already what they called the [GERMAN]-- we only found all that out later on-- where they took the older people and, and trained them to be, be guards on those trains. And we had an older man there, and he came out, not to worry. I remember that, that he told us not to worry, that we go to a labor camp. So that much, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe him when he said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess we believed him for the simple reason that we were out of Auschwitz. We got-- I got hysterical when we ended up in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gross-Rosen, because, you know, and Gross-Rosen looked exactly like Auschwitz, and smaller. And we-- and they had the doors open, that time, so I said, that's it, now. They took us from one place to the other to kill us. But we made it to Merzdorf, which is very close by, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of this guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't know any names from anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a weapon to guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he had a gun. He had a gun. Like a regular gun that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, like all the soldiers wore around--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with your knife that you had when you traded it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you believe it? I had the knife with me all the time, and nobody ever found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hide it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I guess I must have had it underneath somehow or the other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got it through the second sauna, and I got [LAUGHS] it on the train, and I got it to Merzdorf, and I still had it. It's incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how did you get it through the sauna?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that knife went around-- then in Merzdorf, it was a little bit different, because not that we had so much more freedom, but we worked a lot, and the knife came in handy for certain things. So I was the big shot. I had the knife. Nobody else had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you let anyone use it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yeah, of course, if somebody needed a knife or something I would. I remember, what we cut was a-- was-- at the factory was a-- we called it a [GERMAN], which is the stuff they make the thread from for linen, in a weaver-- you know, in weave-- weaveries, or whatever they're called. And we used to open up the flax-- they're, like, about that wide when they grow on the bushes. So we open-- and we got them in the factory to process them, to make a, a yarn from that, right? We open it up, and there were, like, little seeds in it, and we used to eat it. We used to eat that, because it kept us going. We were always hungry. And we knew that nothing could happen if we eat that. So we-- I always had the knife with me to open up those things. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you get the knife through the sauna?

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to take of all your clothes, right? To take a shower?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, but I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were given different clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I don't remember how I got the knife through. I have no idea anymore. But I had it, one way or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your mother do in Merzdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Merzdorf, she was working outside, where they cut the, the flax, or put them together in bundles. And I was working inside, in the-- I don't what you call it, [GERMAN]. Do you know what that is?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Weaving ready-weave material, that's where I worked on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard work. It was military work. We did it for the-- I guess the German army needed linen, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about being separated from your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was like, the, the working hours, and then we were together again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours did you work a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know. Sometimes eight hours, sometimes 10 hours. Time-- sometimes they selected us to do extra work cleaning for the SS. We did that, too. And then we had to do all kind of things. One of the girls were pregnant when she got to Auschwitz, and nobody realized that she was pregnant, was the beginning. And she still was quite-- became quite heavy-- I mean, showing her pregnancy when we were in Merzdorf. And she had her baby, and she died, and the baby died. And I don't know, from no place, they took me and four other girls, in middle of the winter. It was snowing, and ice, and it was terrible. Right in the mountains, and we had to dig the grave and go back to the factory, and they took us on some kind of horse and carriage, carriage, something like that, with the coffin. They had a coffin. And we had to bury her. And her name was Elfi Eiman. I never will forget it. Beautiful young woman. She was married, and she had become pregnant while she was in Theresienstadt. And her husband went to Auschwitz, also. And she never let on that she was pregnant, and that was that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So all kind of experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are any punishments that affected you in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [SIGHS] My mother fainted while we were standing appell outside, from the cold and et cetera. And they took a cold water hose to revive her, but in ice and in snow. And she got very, very, very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was with a few other people who were very sick in one of the sick rooms, whatever they called it, and they had a-- from the Polish girls we had there-- we had Hungarian and Polish girls-- there was a woman who was a doctor. And she was, like, taking care of the sick people. And I still believe that she saved my mother's life. Did she have medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: One way or the other. No medication, but somehow or the other, she got my mother to come around and, and, and get better. But then my mother took second time very sick. I don't remember what she has, because it was weakness, really. And when we were liberated, she was in the sick room at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Merzdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Merzdorf from the end of October, I guess, or beginning of November, whenever we left Auschwitz till May 8, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about six months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A little longer-- yeah, six months, five? Six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the conditions better or worse than Auschwitz? How would you compare?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was better in the respect that you didn't have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that continuous fear, from one hour to the other, what's going to happen? Because you knew you were working, you had to work. The SS women were terrible. They were absolutely terrible. I mean, they were a terror. [BACKROUND NOISE] And it was better in the sense that you had hope, maybe you're going to survive it, and you're going to be liberated. And then on the last months, May-- in April, we heard, already, shooting, and bombing, and things like that. So we always was under the feeling the Russians are coming closer, maybe. Maybe we'll make it, you know. And we were just lucky we didn't get in one of the death march, because a lot of the camps from around there, they were collected and sent towards Czechoslovakia and Austria and other camps again. We made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the food better in Merzdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was better than Auschwitz, but not enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times a day did you get to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we get a piece of bread in the morning and something with-- water, coffee, I don't know what it was supposed to be. And then we got one soup. This was the standard diet for, for every-- any camp, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there barracks similar? Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we weren't in barracks. We were staying in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the factory, you mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that comfortable? I mean, did you have a mattress, covers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- no, we had-- what did we have there? Mattresses, we didn't have, but it looked like a mattress, and it was straw in-- you know, sewn into a piece of material, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was more comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was already a little better than Auschwitz, let's put it that way. And then what we did there is, there were girls who worked like me, in the [GERMAN], like where they make the materials, and they had access to pieces of material. So they smuggled it upstairs, because we were on the last floor. And don't ask me where it came from-- a needle and some yarn and some thread and things. But they're usually the girls who had different type of work than I had. And we cut the pieces of material and made us brassieres and something-- underclothes to wear, et cetera. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the SS women were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. The men were bad. The women were worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: If anything can be worse than an SS man, the women were worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The screaming, the, the hitting-- for no reason, hitting, OK? They were incredible. They were unbelievably bad, and especially the one who called herself the commandant of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think her first name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm not sure. The last name, I never got. But I heard other people calling her [NON-ENGLISH], so, you know, from the SS. I think that was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She hit people. She, she was the one with the hose and hosed my mother up and down with the ice water. And she was terrible. She was just a terror. Two girls tried to escape from Merzdorf, made it out, were hidden by-- in a farm somewhere and then got caught. And they brought the two girls back. What they had to go through by-- what-- how they were mistreated, was unbelievable. They, they weren't killed. I mean, that was a surprise. But how they were mistreated was unbelievable. And all the women, at that particular time, had already a little bit of hair growing back. So all of, of us got shaved again, the whole camp. That must have been, like, in January, February, something like that. Two Czech girls tried to escape. So there were always punishments.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to these two girls when they were caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were liberated. They were liberated with us. I don't know what happened to them afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the punishment that they received?

speaker: interviewee
text: The punishment was that they were-- I mean, they were really beaten up. They were-- I mean, that they were even alive when they came, came back was unbelievable. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw them being beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- yeah, yeah. They were still upstairs in the factory later on with us. And they were two girls which worked outside on the truck and somehow escaped from there. And they were away, like, at least three, four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I could ask somebody, you know, but I forgot. I just know how they looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? Tell me what they looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- originally, were very pretty girls, two very pretty girls. They got very-- you know, with the unshaven heads and everything. But I mean, I can picture them. I know who they were, but the names, I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after they were beaten, did they show the signs of being beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They were black and blue and bloody and, and everything else, everything else, believe me. But that's-- I wish they could have escaped and could have made it back to Czechoslovakia. But they didn't. But they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the only attempt that you know of? People--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only attempt I know of, and the girls were brought back. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you mistreated in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was hit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when you're on that weaving thing, it's like a thing you have to learn. If you don't know it, how the heck would you know how to do things, right? So little by little, we had an idea how to do it. We had to know it. Let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you put, like, it's a capsule, like, about that long, and you put the [GERMAN], which is the thing with the yarn-- you put that inside. And then it goes automatically back and forth, right? And that opened up in the middle of it and tore the whole material apart. And that's when I got it. I was hit very badly. Where were you hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the face, my head, my shoulders, all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they called the Meister. The Meister was the one-- the master from the-- of the-- was actually in charge of that factory-- a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he hit you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he hit me with, like-- what is, what the police walks around with? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Baton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, something like that. Like the wooden baton, something-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you want to tell me about your experience in Merzdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Experiences in Merzdorf? The only experience I can-- nice experience was when I went, on the 8th of May, in the morning, down to my mother into the sick room, because I snuck in there every morning before I had to go to appell and to work to see if she was still alive in the room. And I'm standing, and my mother's bunk bed was right next to a window with a gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had to hurry to look-- we had no mirrors, or anything-- into that window if I could see my hair growing or whatever, right? Vain, even at that time. And I look out, and I see two Russian soldiers on bicycles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I started screaming, the Russians are here. The Russians are here. We are liberated. And they all thought I got nuts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knew even that the SS was gone. They were-- they weren't in the factory anymore. They had left during the night. And we thought we had to come down for appell and go to work. And I saw these two Russian guys on the bicycle, so I ran downstairs screaming, the Russians are here. The Russians are here. [INAUDIBLE] into the courtyard. And maybe a half-- not even a half an hour later, then, the Panzers and the trucks and the, the soldiers came in. And the first thing they did, included the sick people, took us all out of the whole area and were searching the area. And if it was dynamited. It would have taken another two, three hours, we would have [INAUDIBLE] up there. They had dynamite, timed, underneath the factory. Very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who dynamited it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, before they left. But we didn't know that. But obviously, the Russians knew about it from other camps they liberated, you know, from other labor camps they liberated. So we couldn't understand why we had, had to leave immediately. They put us in, in-- you know, it was like-- not a park, but it was joined to a woods-- you know, it was like that. And we, we were standing there, right? And then they came, and they told us that they removed the dynamite and everything, and we went back there. And then they started slaughtering horses and cooking, and that was the first food we got.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do they do with the horses?

speaker: interviewee
text: They slaughtered horses. I don't know if that was their horses or the horses from farmers from over there. And they started cooking in the yard, there. I remember that. And it was the first food, was horse meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it taste?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good, [LAUGHS] believe it or not-- the first meat after all those years, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you recognize the two Russian soldiers? How did you know they were Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just knew. Number one, the Russian uniforms-- they had different uniforms than all the others. And second, they didn't look like Germans to me. That's for sure. Not that I knew what an American uniform would have been, you know? And they had, like, the hats with the red star. That's how I knew it was the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they friendly to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very friendly. They took very-- I mean, I have to say that they took very good care of all of us there. No way about it. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the Russian liberators?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel, finally, to be liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was unbelievable. It really was unbelievable. And then a lot of the German people who lived around that area, all of a sudden, came, brought us clothes and brought us food and wanted us to stay with them, because they were afraid of the Russians. And then from-- we stayed, I think, in Merzdorf, maybe another four, five days, something like that. And we went to [GERMAN], which is the next town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we found out-- I don't know how-- that there was a French prisoner of war camp there. So we figured we'd go there, and maybe can go more west with them. So we went to [GERMAN], and it turned out that the-- we stayed a few days in the prisoner of war camp there. And then somehow or the other, in the surrounding over there were a lot of labor camps, so all of a sudden, all these Jewish prisoners, women and men, showed up. And they, somehow or the other, must have been directly in the [GERMAN] area, because they knew their way around, so they got us apartments.

speaker: interviewer
